entries.ocr_text:
PROCLAMATION
TO THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY OF THE
COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY:
WHEREAS, The budgetary needs of the several depart
ments, boards, commissions and agencies of the government of
Kentucky for the two years beginning July 1, 1936, have not yet
been determined by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky; and
WHEREAS, Such budgetry needs must be determined before
the amount of revenue to meet such needs can be ascertained:
NOW, THEREFORE, In order to determine the budgetary
needs of the several departments, boards, commissions and
agencies of Kentucky, and according to the power vested in me
by the Constitution of Kentucky to convene the General As
sembly into Extraordinary Session, I do issue this, my proclama
tion, convening the General Assembly of Kentucky in Extraor
dinary Session at the seat of government at Frankfort, Ken
tucky, at two o'clock P. M., on Monday, March 9, 1936, for the
sole purpose of considering the following subject:
(1) To consider, determine and prepare a budget which
will meet the needs of the several departments, boards, commis
sions and agencies of the government of the Commonwealth of
Kentucky, for a period of two years from July 1, 1936, and only
said subject.
You will therefore in accordance with this proclamation,
assemble in Extraordinary Session at the seat of government
at Frankfort, Kentucky, on the day and date above written, to
consider the subject herein mentioned and only said subject.
Given under my hand at 6:05 o'clock P. M., this 7th day of
March, 1936.
By the Governor: (Signed) A. B. CHANDLER,
Governor of Kentucky.
(Signed) CHAS. D. ARNETT,
Secretary of State.
[SEAL]
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, Extraordinary Session, 1936, p. 12

classification.title: Proclamation Convening General Assembly into Extraordinary Session
classification.summary: This document is a proclamation by the Governor of Kentucky, A. B. Chandler, convening the General Assembly into an extraordinary session on March 9, 1936. The sole purpose of this session is to determine the budgetary needs of the state's departments, boards, commissions, and agencies for the two years beginning July 1, 1936, and to prepare a budget to meet those needs.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is a proclamation from the Governor of Kentucky calling for an extraordinary session of the General Assembly. The purpose of this session is explicitly stated as determining budgetary needs and preparing a budget. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any other element typically associated with Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with high confidence. The category is null as it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'other' as it is a proclamation, not a legislative act itself. The racial indicator is 'none' as race is not mentioned.

classification.title: Chapter 143
classification.summary: This statute details the financial management of the Department of Welfare in Kentucky for the year 1936. It outlines how receipts from convict labor and other sources are to be paid into the State Treasury as a revolving fund. It specifies the purposes for which withdrawals can be made from this fund, including the treatment of the insane, feeble-minded, and epileptic wards, and the operation of various state institutions such as the State Reformatory, State Penitentiary, and state hospitals. It also allocates funds for the purchase of land and erection of buildings for convict confinement and mental health facilities, and for cooperation with the Federal Government under the Social Security Act and administration of Old Age Pensions. A specific allocation is made for the Kentucky Children's Home "Society for White Children."

hausted, and whereas, the work of the 1936 General

entries.ocr_text:
PROCLAMATION
TO THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY OP THE
COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
WHEREAS, The anticipated revenues from existing tax
laws are inadequate to finance the several departments, boards,
commissions and agencies of the government of Kentucky for
the biennial period beginning July 1, 1936; and
WHEREAS, The State budget for said biennial period can
not be balanced without additional revenues; and
WHEREAS, An appropriation act has already been passed
by the General Assembly setting forth the financial needs of the
several departments, boards, commissions and agencies of the
State government; and
WHEREAS, The existing tax laws cannot reasonably be
expected to produce more than eleven million five hundred thou
sand dollars ($11,500,000.00) for each of the years of the biennial
period beginning July 1, 1936; and
NOW THEREFORE, In order to raise the necessary reve
nues to balance the State budget and meet the financial needs
of the several departments, boards, commissions and agencies
of the State of Kentucky, and according to the power vested in
me by the Constitution of Kentucky to convene the General
Assembly into Extraordinary Session, I do issue this, my procla
mation, convening the General Assembly of Kentucky, at two
o'clock p. m. on Monday, March 30, 1936, for the sole purpose
of considering the following subject:
(1) To pass laws raising the necessary revenues, in addi
tion to those anticipated from existing tax laws, to balance the
State budget and meet the financial needs of the several de
partments, boards, commissions and agencies of the government
of the Commonwealth of Kentucky for a period of two years
beginning July 1, 1936, and only said subject.
You will therefore in accordance with this proclamation,
assemble in Extraordinary Session at the seat of government at
Frankfort, Kentucky, on the day and date above written, to con
sider the subject herein mentioned and only said subject.
Given under my hand at 3:25 o'clock p. m., this 27th day of
March, 1936.
By the Governor: A. B. CHANDLER
Governor of Kentucky
CHARLES D. ARNETT
Secretary of State

CHAPTER 2.
AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation for the sale of vinous
and spirituous liquors repealing, amending and re-enacting
the last paragraph of Section six (6), Article IV, Chapter one
hundred and forty-six (146), Acts of the General Assembly
of one thousand nine hundred thirty-four (1934), codified as
Section two thousand five hundred fifty-four (2554) b-sixteen
(16), Baldwin's Supplement to Carroll's Kentucky Statutes,
and declaring an emergency.
Be it enacted by the Commonwealth of Kentucky :
Section One (1). There is hereby imposed a tax
of seven cents (7c) upon the sale or distribution by
gift or sale of spirituous liquors in containers of
half pint and less in size, except as hereinafter pro
vided.
Section Two (2). The last paragraph of Section
six (6), Article IV, Chapter one hundred and forty-
six (146), of the Acts of the General Assembly of
one thousand nine hundred thirty-four (1934), is
hereby repealed, amended and re-enacted so that
when re-enacted, it shall read as follows : "All such
sales shall be in unbroken package in either quart,
one fifth gallon, pint, and half -pint sizes, and shall
not be consumed on the premises except in the case
of spirituous liquors which are contained in two-
ounce (2 oz.) or less containers, that said containers
shall not be sold by any licensed dispensary unless
such dispensary has obtained a license to sell spirit
uous liquor by the drink. That the tax levied upon
spirituous liquor contained in two ounce (2 oz.) con
tainers or containers less in size shall be two cents
(2c) upon each such container of spirituous liquor."

It also provides a discount for distributors to cover breakage and leakage.

classification.title: An Act to Amend an Act Entitled "An Act to Provide for the Levy and Collection of a Tax on the Sale of Malt Beverages", Approved March 17, 1934, and Declaring an Emergency.
classification.summary: This act details the powers of the Department of Revenue in enforcing a tax on malt beverages. It grants the department the authority to examine witnesses, inspect records and inventory of businesses involved in the sale of malt beverages, institute legal proceedings for tax collection, and attach property or garnish debtors without bond under certain conditions. It also establishes the confidentiality of information obtained during these investigations.

classification.title: An Act relating to the use and reuse of tax stamps or crowns.
classification.summary: This act outlines penalties for the fraudulent use, sale, or possession of tax stamps or crowns, including fines and imprisonment. It also establishes a 100% penalty for failing to properly affix stamps or crowns within the prescribed time, to be collected by the Department of Revenue. Commodities found unstamped are considered prima facie evidence of intent for sale or use in the state. Manufacturers may defer tax payment on inventoried taxable commodities with Department of Revenue approval.

26 Acts of the General Assembly
Section Fourteen (14). No suit shall be main
tained in any court to restrain or delay the collec
tion or payment of the tax levied by this Act. The
aggrieved taxpayer shall pay under protest the tax
as and when required and may at any time within
two years from the date of such payment sue the
Commonwealth through its agent, the Auditor of
Public Accounts, in an action at law in any court,
state or federal, otherwise having jurisdiction of
the parties and subject matter, for the recovery of
the tax paid with legal interest thereon from the
date of payment. If it is finally determined that
said tax or any part thereof was wrongfully col
lected, for any reason, it shall be the duty of the
Auditor of Public Accounts to issue his warrant on
the Treasurer of the Commonwealth of Kentucky
for the amount of such tax so adjudged to have been
wrongfully collected together with legal interest
thereon. The Treasurer shall pay the same at once
out of the General Expenditure Fund of the State
in preference to other warrants or claims against
the Commonwealth. A separate suit need not be
filed for each individual payment made by any tax
payer, but a recovery may be had in one suit for as
many payments as may have been made.
Section Fifteen (15). If commodities, the sale of
which is made taxable by this Act, are distributed
under such circumstances that the Constitution and
laws of the United States or of Kentucky preclude
the possibilities of imposing the tax, then such tax
shall not apply. In case of sales made into another
state, the Department of Revenue may dispense
with stamps or crowns or may require stamps on
cases or may make appropriate regulations pre
scribing some other means whereby the tax may not
be made payable.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, Extraordinary Session, 1936, p. 127

The statute was enacted in 1936.

Section Twenty-three (23). Separability clause.
The provisions of this Act are severable, and if any
of its provisions or any penalty provided by this
Act shall be held unconstitutional, such provision
or the particular penalty held to be invalid shall not
affect or impair the remainder of the Act or such
part of the penalties as are not held to be invalid
and the decisions of the courts shall not affect or
impair the remaining provisions of this Act or any
of the other penalties anywhere in this Act pro
vided. It is hereby declared as a legislative intent
that this Act would have been adopted had any un
constitutional provisions of such invalid penalties
not been included herein.
Section Twenty-four (24). Emergency. Where
as, the present State revenue does not meet the im
mediate needs for maintenance of the State govern
ment an emergency is declared to exist and this Act
shall become a law and be effective upon its pas
sage and approval by the Governor.

classification.summary: This statute requires individuals and entities subject to Kentucky jurisdiction, including lessees, mortgagors, fiduciaries, and employers, to file an annual income tax return with the Department of Revenue if they have made payments of $600 or more in income to another person domiciled in the state. It also allows the Department to require information returns for non-residents and for payments of dividends or interest on corporate obligations, regardless of amount. The statute exempts interest payments on obligations of the United States or the Commonwealth of Kentucky. It also allows the Department to require a copy of the federal return when deemed necessary.

Whenever a resident individual of this State has

classification.title: Credits allowed non-residents
classification.summary: This section outlines the conditions under which a non-resident individual who has paid income tax to their home state on income derived from Kentucky can receive a credit for that tax against their Kentucky income tax liability. The credit is allowed only if the non-resident's home state offers a similar credit to Kentucky residents or taxes income from Kentucky while exempting income from their own state for Kentucky residents. No credit is allowed for income that is exempt in the non-resident's home state.

As soon as practicable after each return is re
ceived, the Department shall examine and audit
it. If the amount of tax computed by the Depart

complete return in which case the excess may be as

the State Tax Commission for a hearing. The Tax
Commission shall set a date within a reasonable
time for a public hearing; and, on the basis of the
law and the facts, the Tax Commission shall sustain
the original assessment or make a new assessment.

prima facie evidence of the amount of the tax due,

notice of the assessment of tax, interest, and penal
ties. The taxpayer shall remit to the Tax Commis
sion within sixty (60) days. Nothing herein shall
prevent the taxpayer from appealing by petition in

cuit Court, from the decision by the Tax Commis
sion. Either party may appeal to the Court of

Section Thirty-Three (33). Tax a personal debt.
Every tax imposed by this. Act, and all increases,
interest and penalties thereon, shall become, from
the time it is due and payable, a personal debt, from
the person or persons liable to pay the same, to the
Commonwealth of Kentucky.
Section Thirty-Four (34). Penalty and interest.
If taxes imposed by this Act be not paid when due,
as provided herein, the taxpayer liable to such tax
shall pay as a penalty, in addition to the amount
of such tax, and additional tax of five per cent (5%)
of such amount, plus one per cent (1%) for each
month the tax or additional tax remains unpaid;
but the Department, upon submission to it of satis
factory evidence that the failure to pay such taxes
within the time prescribed in this Act was not willful
or evasive, may modify the exaction to not less than
one per cent (1%) for each month following the
due date of the tax.

delay. No suit shall be maintained in any court to
restrain or delay the collection or payment of the

pay the tax under protest as and when required by

Commonwealth through its agent, the Auditor of
Public Accounts, in an action at law in any court,
State or Federal, otherwise having jurisdiction of
the parties and subject matter, for the recovery of
the tax paid with legal interest thereon from the
date of payment. If it is finally determined that
said tax or any part thereof was wrongfully col
lected, for any reason, it shall be the duty of the
Auditor of Public Accounts to issue his warrant on
the Treasurer of the Commonwealth of Kentucky
for the amount of such tax so adjudged to have been
wrongfully collected together with legal interest
thereon. The Treasurer shall pay the same at once
out of the General Expenditure Fund of the State
in preference to other warrants or claims against
the Commonwealth. A separate suit need not be
filed for each individual payment made by any tax
payer, but a recovery may be had in one suit for as
many payments as may have been made, so far as

The provisions of this Act are severable. If any
provisions, section, paragraph, or the application
of any provision to any class of taxpayers should be
held unconstitutional or invalid, such decision shall
not affect or impair the remainder of the Act, it
being hereby declared the legislative intent to tax
separately each class of income contemplated by
any of its provisions.
Section Thirty-Nine (39). Income for first taxable
year. The taxes imposed by this Act shall first be
collected and paid with respect to the net income
received during the calendar year 1936. The income
taxable during the first taxable year, in the event
the taxpayer's taxable year is a fiscal year, shall be
deemed to be that fraction of the income for the en
tire fiscal year equal to the number of days remain
ing in the fiscal year after January one (1), one
thousand nine hundred thirty-six (1936) divided
by three hundred and sixty-five (365). Provided,
however, that if the taxpayer's records disclose the
exact income for that part of the fiscal year falling
in the calendar year 1936 then the portion of the
fiscal year's income taxable hereunder shall be that
portion received or accrued during the calendar
year 1936.

classification.title: AN ACT relating to the licensing of motor vehicle operators
classification.summary: This act establishes regulations for the licensing of motor vehicle operators in Kentucky. It defines terms, outlines licensing requirements, specifies exemptions for certain individuals and non-residents, sets age limits for school bus and public passenger vehicle drivers, and details procedures for instruction permits, temporary licenses, applications, and the maintenance of operator registers. The act also covers license issuance, duplicates, signing and carrying licenses, expiration, reporting of convictions and suspensions, mandatory suspension or revocation, hearings and appeals, new licenses after revocation, and liability for negligence of minors. It also addresses violations, perjury for false affidavits, and penalties for misdemeanors and driving with a suspended or revoked license, and establishes prima facie evidence of negligence in accidents. The act includes provisions for its short title, constitutionality, repeal, and an emergency clause.

classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the issuance and suspension of motor vehicle operator's licenses based on various personal incapacities and legal statuses. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The criteria for license denial or suspension are based on individual fitness to operate a vehicle safely and legally, irrespective of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 15.
AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation and declaring an
emergency.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky.
Section One (1). Definitions, (a) "Places of
amusement and/or entertainment" shall mean and
include all theatres, ball parks and stadia, lecture
halls, dance halls, night clubs, skating rinks, public
bathing places, and all places of other sorts, whether
like the above mentioned or not, at which businesses
providing amusements and/or entertainments for
which a charge is made, are operated, but shall not
mean nor include race tracks subject to the super
vision of the Kentucky Pacing Commission.
(b) The term "sale" shall mean and include the
transfer or exchange for valuable consideration of
services of all classes and kinds.
(c) The word "person" shall mean and include
individuals, firms, joint stock companies, syndicates
and corporations.
(d) "Professional entertainers" shall mean and
include any person or persons who receive pay in
money or money's worth for their performances.
(e) "Proprietor" shall mean and include any
person who owns, operates, or manages a place of
amusement and/or entertainment.
(f) "Department" shall mean and include the
Department of Revenue of Kentucky.

Section One (1). Definitions :
(a) The word "person" as used in this Act in
cludes individuals, co-partnerships, business trusts,
joint stock companies, and corporations.
(b) The term "gross receipts" as used in this
Act shall be construed to mean the amount received
in money, credits, property, or other money's worth,
regardless of whether or not the form thereof be
enumerated herein. Provided, that in case the busi
ness is conducted on credit the consideration shall

be made in cash, property, or other money's worth,
regardless of whether or not the form be numerated
herein.

cludes all persons required hereunder to make re
ports and pay the tax or taxes imposed by this Act.
(d) The term "Department" as used in this
Act shall mean the Department of Revenue of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky.
(e) The word "sale" as used in this Act in
cludes the transfer of services for valuable consid
eration as well as similar transfers of property.
